english_head: I assign rhetoric; but without noticing that I have not yet answered that, he proceeds to ask whether I do not consider it a fine thing. But I am not going to reply to the question whether I consider rhetoric a fine or a base thing, until I have first answered what it is; for it would not be fair, Polus: but if you want the information, ask me what sort of branch of flattery I assert rhetoric to be. I ask you then; so answer, what sort of branch it is. Now, will you understand when I answer? Rhe

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἂν μάθοις ἀποκριναμένου ἔστιν γὰρ ἡ ῥητορικὴ κατὰ τὸν ἐμὸν λόγον πολιτικῆς μορίου εἴδωλον
greek_tokens: ara oun an manqanw apokrinw eimi gar o rhtorikos kata o emos logos politikos morion eidwlon tis oun kalos h aisxros legw autos eimi aisxros egw o gar kakos aisxros kalew epei deomai su apokrinw ws hdh oida o egw legos ma o dios eimi swkraths alla egw oude autos sunihmi ostis legw
english_head: by my account, is a semblance of a branch of politics. Well then, do you call it a fine or a base thing? A base one, I call it—for all that is bad I call base—since I am to answer you as though you had already understood my meaning. Nor do I myself, upon my word, Socrates,

greek_head: Εἰκότως γε ὦ Γοργία οὐδὲν γάρ πω σαφὲς λέγω Πῶλος δὲ ὅδε νέος ἐστὶ καὶ ὀξύς
greek_tokens: eoikotws ge eimi gorgihs oudeis gar pw safhs legos pwlos de ode neos eimi kai ocus alla outos men ea egw de eipon pws legw politikos morion eidwlon eimi o rhtorikos alla egw peiraw frazw o ge egw fainw eimi o rhtorikos ei de mh tugxanw eimi outos pwlos ode
english_head: grasp your meaning either. And no wonder, Gorgias, for as yet my statement is not at all clear; but Polus here is so young and fresh! Ah, do not mind him; but tell me what you mean by rhetoric being a semblance of a branch of politics. Well, I will try to express what rhetoric appears to me to be: if it is not in fact what I say, Polus here will refute me. There are things, I suppose, that you call body and soul?

greek_head: ἐλέγξει σῶμά που καλεῖς τι καὶ ψυχήν Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Οὐκοῦν καὶ τούτων οἴει τινὰ εἶναι ἑκατέρου εὐεξίαν
greek_tokens: elegcis swma pou kalew tis kai yuxh pws gar ou oukoun kai outos oiomai tis eimi ekateros euecia egw tis de dokew men euecia eimi de ou oios toiosde legos polus dokew eu exw o swma os ou an radios aisqanomai tis ostis ou eu exw alla h iatros te kai o gumnastikos tis alhqhs legw o toioutos legos kai en swma eimi kai en yuxw o poiew men dokew eu exw o swma kai o yuxh
english_head: Of course. And each of these again you believe to have a good condition? I do. And again, a good condition that may seem so, but is not? As an example, let me give the following: many people seem to be in good bodily condition when it would not be easy for anyone but a doctor, or one of the athletic trainers, to perceive that they are not so. You are right. Something of this sort I say there is in body and in soul, which makes the body or the soul seem to be in good condition, though it is none

greek_head: ἔχει δὲ οὐδὲν μᾶλλον Ἔστι ταῦτα Φέρε δή σοι ἐὰν δύνωμαι σαφέστερον ἐπιδείξω ὃ
greek_tokens: exis de oudeis mallon eimi outos ferw dh su ean dunamai safhs epideiknumi o legos duo eimi o pragma duo legos texnh o men epi o yuxw politikos kalew o de epi swma eis men outws onomazw ou exw su eis de eimi o o swma qerapeia duo morion legos o men gumnastikos o de iatrikos o de politikos anti men o gumnastikos o nomoqetikos antistrofos de o iatrikos o dikaiosunh
english_head: Quite so. Now let me see if I can explain my meaning to you more clearly. There are two different affairs to which I assign two different arts: the one, which has to do with the soul, I call politics; the other, which concerns the body, though I cannot give you a single name for it offhand, is all one business, the tendance of the body, which I can designate in two branches as gymnastic and medicine. Under politics I set legislation in the place of gymnastic, and justice to match medicine.

greek_head: ἐπικοινωνοῦσι μὲν δὴ ἀλλήλαις ἅτε περὶ τὸ αὐτὸ οὖσαι ἑκάτεραι τούτων ἥ τε ἰατρικὴ τῇ γυμναστικῇ καὶ ἡ δικαιοσύνη
greek_tokens: epikoinwnew men dh allhlwn ate peri o autos eimi ekateros outos o te iatrikos o gumnastikos kai o dikaiosunh o nomoqetikos omws de diaferw tis allhlwn tessares dh outos eimi kai aei pros o beltistos qerapeuw o men o swma o de o yuxh o kolakeutikos aisqanomai ou gignwskw legos alla stoxazomai tetraxa eautou dianemw upoduomai upo ekastos o morion prospoiew
english_head: In each of these pairs, of course—medicine and gymnastic, justice and legislation—there is some intercommunication, as both deal with the same thing; at the same time they have certain differences. Now these four, which always bestow their care for the best advantage respectively of the body and the soul, are noticed by the art of flattery which, I do not say with knowledge, but by speculation, divides herself into four parts, and then, insinuating herself into each of those branches,

greek_head: εἶναι τοῦτο ὅπερ ὑπέδυ καὶ τοῦ μὲν βελτίστου οὐδὲν φροντίζει τῷ δὲ ἀεὶ ἡδίστῳ θηρεύεται τὴν ἄνοιαν καὶ ἐξαπατᾷ ὥστε
greek_tokens: eimi outos osper upoduomai kai o men beltistos oudeis frontizw o de aei hdus qhreuw o anoia kai ecapataw wste dokew pleistos acios eimi upo men oun o iatrikos o oyopoiikos upoduomai kai prospoiew o beltistos sition o swma oida wste ei dew en pais diagwnizomai oyopoios te kai iatros h en anhr outws anohtos wsper e pais poteros epaiw peri o xrhstos siteomai kai
english_head: pretends to be that into which she has crept, and cares nothing for what is the best, but dangles what is most pleasant for the moment as a bait for folly, and deceives it into thinking that she is of the highest value. Thus cookery assumes the form of medicine, and pretends to know what foods are best for the body; so that if a cook and a doctor had to contend before boys, or before men as foolish as boys, as to which of the two, the doctor or the cook, understands the question of sound and nox

greek_head: πονηρῶν ὁ ἰατρὸς ἢ ὁ ὀψοποιός λιμῷ ἂν ἀποθανεῖν τὸν ἰατρόν κολακείαν μὲν οὖν αὐτὸ καλῶ καὶ αἰσχρόν φημι
greek_tokens: ponhros o iatros h o oyopoios limos an apoqnhskw o iatros kolakeia men oun autos kalew kai aisxros fhmi
english_head: the doctor would starve to death. Flattery, however, is what I call it,

greek_head: εἶναι τὸ τοιοῦτον ὦ Πῶλε τοῦτο γὰρ πρὸς σὲ λέγω ὅτι τοῦ ἡδέος στοχάζεται ἄνευ τοῦ βελτίστου τέχνην δὲ αὐτὴν
greek_tokens: eimi o toioutos eimi pwlos outos gar pros sos legos ostis o hdus stoxazomai aneu o beltistos texnh de autos ou fhmi eimi alla empeiria ostis ou exis logos oudeis os prosferw o prosferw opoios atta o fus eimi wste o aitios ekastos mh exw eipon egw de texnh ou kalew o an eimi alogos pragma outos de peri ei amfisbhtew eqelw upexw logos
english_head: and I say that this sort of thing is a disgrace, Polus—for here I address you—because it aims at the pleasant and ignores the best; and I say it is not an art, but a habitude, since it has no account to give of the real nature of the things it applies, and so cannot tell the cause of any of them. I refuse to give the name of art to anything that is irrational: if you dispute my views, I am ready to give my reasons.

greek_head: Τῇ μὲν οὖν ἰατρικῇ ὥσπερ λέγω ἡ ὀψοποιικὴ κολακεία ὑπόκειται τῇ δὲ γυμναστικῇ κατὰ τὸν αὐτὸν τρόπον τοῦτον
greek_tokens: o men oun iatrikos wsper legos o oyopoiikos kolakeia upokeimai o de gumnastikos kata o autos tropos outos o kommwtikos kakourgos te kai apathlos kai agennhs kai aneleuqeros sxhma kai xrwma kai leioths kai esqos apataw wste poiew allotrios kallos efelkw o oikew o dia o gumnastikos amelew ina oun mh makrologew eqelw su eipon wsper e gewmetrhs hdh gar
english_head: However, as I put it, cookery is flattery disguised as medicine; and in just the same manner self-adornment personates gymnastic: with its rascally, deceitful, ignoble, and illiberal nature it deceives men by forms and colors, polish and dress so as to make them, in the effort of assuming an extraneous beauty, neglect the native sort that comes through gymnastic. Well, to avoid prolixity, I am willing to put it to you like a geometer—for by this time I expect you can follow me:

greek_head: ἂν ἴσως ἀκολουθήσαις ὅτι ὃ κομμωτικὴ πρὸς γυμναστικήν τοῦτο σοφιστικὴ πρὸς νομοθετικήν καὶ ὅτι ὃ ὀψοποιικὴ πρὸς
greek_tokens: an isos akolouqew ostis o kommwtikos pros gumnastikos outos sofistikos pros nomoqetikos kai ostis o oyopoiikos pros iatrikos outos rhtorikos pros dikaiosunh osper mentoi legos diisthmi men outws fusis ate de eggus eimi furw en o autos kai peri tautos sofisths kai rhtwr kai ou exw ostis xraw oute autos eautou oute e allos anqrwpos outos kai gar an ei mh o yuxh o swma
english_head: as self-adornment is to gymnastic, so is sophistry to legislation; and as cookery is to medicine, so is rhetoric to justice. But although, as I say, there is this natural distinction between them, they are so nearly related that sophists and orators are jumbled up as having the same field and dealing with the same subjects, and neither can they tell what to make of each other, nor the world at large what to make of them. For indeed, if the soul were not in command of the body, but the latter had

greek_head: ἐπεστάτει ἀλλ' αὐτὸ αὑτῷ καὶ μὴ ὑπὸ ταύτης κατεθεωρεῖτο καὶ διεκρίνετο ἥ τε ὀψοποιικὴ καὶ ἡ ἰατρική ἀλλ' αὐτὸ τὸ
greek_tokens: epistatew alla autos eautou kai mh upo outos kataqewrew kai diakrinw o te oyopoiikos kai o iatrikos alla autos o swma krinw staqmaomai o xaris o pros eautou o o anacagorhs an polus eimi eimi filos pwlos su gar outos empeiros omos an pas xrhma furw en o autos akritos eimi o te iatrikos kai ugieinos kai oyopoiikos o men oun egw fhmi o rhtorikos eimi akouw antistrofos
english_head: and distinguished by the soul, but the body itself were the judge, forming its own estimate of them by the gratifications they gave it, we should have a fine instance of what Anaxagoras described, my dear Polus,—for you are versed in these matters: everything would be jumbled together, without distinction as between medicinal and healthful and tasty concoctions. Well now, you have heard what I state rhetoric to be—the counterpart of cookery in the soul, acting here as that does on the body. It m

greek_head: ὀψοποιίας ἐν ψυχῇ ὡς ἐκεῖνο ἐν σώματι ἴσως μὲν οὖν ἄτοπον πεποίηκα ὅτι σε οὐκ ἐῶν μακροὺς λόγους
greek_tokens: oyopoiia en yuxw ws ekeinos en swma isos men oun atopos poiew ostis su ou eaw makros logos legw autos suxnos logos apoteinw acios men oun egw suggnwmh exw eimi legw gar egw braxus ou manqanw oude xraomai o apokrisls os su apokrinw oudeis oios su eimi alla dew dihghsis ean men oun kai
english_head: when I do not allow you to make long speeches, to have extended mine to so considerable a length. However, I can fairly claim indulgence: for when I spoke briefly you did not understand me; you were unable to make any use of the answer I gave you, but required a full exposition. Now if I on my part cannot tell what use to make of any answers you may give me, you shall extend your speech also;

greek_head: ἐγὼ σοῦ ἀποκρινομένου μὴ ἔχω ὅτι χρήσωμαι ἀπότεινε καὶ σὺ λόγον ἐὰν δὲ ἔχω ἔα με χρῆσθαι δίκαιον γάρ καὶ
greek_tokens: egw sew apokrinw mh exw ostis xraw apoteinw kai su logos ean de exw ea egw xraomai dikaios gar kai nun outos o apokrisls ei tis exis xraomai xraomai tis oun fhmi kolakeia dokew su eimi o rhtorikos kolakeia men oun egw eipon morion alla ou mnhmoneuw thlikoutos eimi eimi pwlos tis taxa draw ara oun dokew su ws kolac en o polis faulos nomizw e agaqos rhtwr
english_head: but if I can make some use of them, allow me to do it; that will only be fair. And now, if you can make any use of this answer of mine, do so. Then what is it you say? Do you take rhetoric to be flattery? Well, I said rather a branch of flattery. Why, at your age, Polus, have you no memory? What will you do later on? Then do you think that good orators are considered to be flatterers in their cities, and so worthless?

greek_head: Ἐρώτημα τοῦτ' ἐρωτᾷς ἢ λόγου τινὸς ἀρχὴν λέγεις Ἐρωτῶ ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: erwthma outos erwtaw h logos tis arxh legw erwtaw egw oude nomizw egw dokew pws ou nomizw ou megas dunamai en o polis ou ei o dunamai ge legw agaqos tis eimi o dunamai alla meis legos ge elaxistos toinun egw dokew o en o polis dunamai e rhtwr tis de ou wsper e turannos apokteinw te
english_head: Is that a question you are asking, or are you beginning a speech? I am asking a question. To my mind, they are not considered at all. How not considered? Have they not the chief power in their cities? No, if you mean power in the sense of something good for him who has it. Why, of course I mean that. Then, to my thinking, the orators have the smallest power of all who are in their city.

greek_head: ὃν ἂν βούλωνται καὶ ἀφαιροῦνται χρήματα καὶ ἐκβάλλουσιν ἐκ τῶν πόλεων ὃν ἂν δοκῇ αὐτοῖς
greek_tokens: os an boulomai kai afairew xrhma kai ekballw ek o polis os an dokazw autos nh o kuwn amfignoew mentoi eimi pwlos epi ekastos os legw poteros autos outos legw kai gnwmh sautou apofainw h egw erwtaw alla egw sos erwtaw eien eimi filos epeita duo ama egw erwtaw pws duo ou arti outws pws legw eimi ou apokteinw
english_head: What? Are they not like the despots, in putting to death anyone they please, and depriving anyone of his property and expelling him from their cities as they may think fit? By the Dog, I fear I am still in two minds, Polus, at everything you say, as to whether this is a statement on your own part, and a declaration of your own opinion, or a question you are putting to me. Why, I am asking you. Very well, my friend then are you asking me two things at once? How two?

greek_head: οἱ ῥήτορες οὓς ἂν βούλωνται ὥσπερ οἱ τύραννοι καὶ χρήματα ἀφαιροῦνται καὶ ἐξελαύνουσιν ἐκ τῶν πόλεων ὃν ἂν δοκῇ
greek_tokens: e rhtwr os an boulomai wsper e turannos kai xrhma afairew kai ecelaunw ek o polis os an dokazw autos egw legos toinun su ostis duo outos eimi o erwthma kai apokrinw ge su pros amfoteros fhmi gar eimi pwlos egw kai o rhtwr kai o turannos dunamai men en o polis mikros wsper nundi elegos oudeis
english_head: Were you not this moment saying something like this: Is it not the case that the orators put to death anyone they wish, like the despots, and deprive people of property and expel them from their cities as they may think fit? I was. Then I tell you that there are two questions here, and I will give you answers to them both. For I say, Polus, that the orators and the despots alike have the least power in their cities, as I stated just now; since they do nothing

greek_head: γὰρ ποιεῖν ὧν βούλονται ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν ποιεῖν μέντοι ὅτι ἂν αὐτοῖς δόξῃ βέλτιστον εἶναι
greek_tokens: gar poiew os boulomai ws epos eipon poiew mentoi ostis an autos doca beltistos eimi oukoun outos eimi o megas dunamai ou os ge fhmi pwlos egw ou fhmi fhmi men oun egw ma o ou su ge epei o megas dunamai fhmi agaqos eimi o dunamai fhmi gar oun agaqos oun oiomai eimi ean tis poiew outos o an dokazw autos beltistos eimi new mh exw kai outos kalew su megas dunamai ou egw oukoun apodeicis o rhtwr new exw kai
english_head: that they wish to do, practically speaking, though they do whatever they think to be best. Well, and is not that a great power to have? No, judging at least by what Polus says. I say no! Pardon me, I say yes. No, by the ————, you do not; for you said that great power is a good to him who has it. Yes, and I maintain it. Then do you regard it as a good, when a man does what he thinks to be best, without having intelligence? Is that what you call having a great power? No, I do not. Then will you pr

greek_head: τέχνην τὴν ῥητορικὴν ἀλλὰ μὴ κολακείαν ἐμὲ ἐξελέγξας εἰ δέ με ἐάσεις ἀνέλεγκτον οἱ ῥήτορες οἱ ποιοῦντες ἐν ταῖς
greek_tokens: texnh o rhtorikos alla mh kolakeia egw ecelegxw ei de egw eaw anelegktos e rhtwr e poiew en o polis o dokew autos kai e turannos oudeis agaqos outos ktaomai o de dunamis eimi ws su fhmi agaqos o de poiew aneu neomai o dokew kai su omologew kakos eimi h ou egw pws an oun e rhtwr megas dunamai h e turannos en o polis ean mh swkratew ecelegxw upo pwlhs ostis poiew o boulomai
english_head: Else, if you are going to leave me unrefuted, the orators who do what they think fit in their cities, and the despots, will find they have got no good in doing that, if indeed power is, as you say, a good, but doing what one thinks fit without intelligence is—as you yourself admit, do you not?—an evil. Yes, I do. How then can the orators or the despots have great power in their cities, unless Socrates is refuted by Polus, and admits that they do what they wish?

greek_head: Οὗτος ἀνήρ Οὔ φημι ποιεῖν αὐτοὺς ἃ βούλονται ἀλλά μ' ἔλεγχε
greek_tokens: outos anhr ou fhmi poiew autos o boulomai alla egw elegxos ou arti omologew poiew o dokew autos beltistos eimi outos prosqen kai gar nun omologew ou oun poiew o boulomai ou fhmi poiew o dokew autos fhmi sxetlios ge legw kai uperfuhs eimi swkraths mh kakhgorew eimi lwstos pwlos ina proseipon su
english_head: Hark at the man————! I deny that they do what they wish: there, refute me. Did you not admit just now that they do what they think best? Yes, and I admit it now. Then do they not do what they wish? I say no. When they do what they think fit? Yes. What shocking, nay, monstrous answers, Socrates!

greek_head: κατὰ σέ ἀλλ' εἰ μὲν ἔχεις ἐμὲ ἐρωτᾶν ἐπίδειξον ὅτι ψεύδομαι εἰ δὲ μή αὐτὸς ἀποκρίνου
greek_tokens: kata sos alla ei men exis egw erwtaw epideiknumi ostis yeudw ei de mh autos apokrinw alla eqelw apokrinw ina kai oida ostis legw poteros oun su dokew e anqrwpos outos boulomai o an prassw ekastote h ekeinos e eneka prassw outos o prassw oios e o farmakon pinw para o iatros poteros su dokew outos boulomai osper poiew pinw o farmakon kai algew h ekeinos o ugiainw e eneka pinw dhlos ostis o
english_head: Spare your invective, peerless Polus—if I may address you in your own style: but if you have a question to ask me, expose my error otherwise, make answer yourself. Well, I am ready to answer, in order that I may know what you mean. Then is it your view that people wish merely that which they do each time, or that which is the object of their doing what they do? For instance, do those who take medicine by doctor’s orders wish, in your opinion, merely what they do,—to take the medicine and suffer

greek_head: ὑγιαίνειν Οὐκοῦν καὶ οἱ πλέοντές τε καὶ τὸν ἄλλον χρηματισμὸν χρηματιζόμενοι οὐ τοῦτό ἐστιν ὃ βούλονται ὃ
greek_tokens: ugiainw oukoun kai e plew te kai o allos xrhmatismos xrhmatizw ou outos eimi o boulomai o poiew ekastote tis gar boulomai plew te kai kinduneuw kai pragma exw alla ekeinos oimh e eneka plew ploutew ploutos gar eneka plew panu ge allos tis oun outws kai peri pas ean tis tis prassw eneka tis ou outos boulomai o prassw alla ekeinos
english_head: To be healthy, without a doubt. And so with seafarers and such as pursue profit generally in trade; what they wish is not what they are doing at each moment—for who wishes to go on a voyage, and incur all its danger and trouble? It is rather, I conceive, the object of their voyage—to get wealth; since it is for wealth that they go on it. Certainly. And is it not just the same in every case? If a man does something for an object, he does not wish the thing that he does, but the thing for which he

greek_head: οὗ ἕνεκα πράττει Ναί Ἆρ' οὖν ἔστιν τι τῶν ὄντων ὃ οὐχὶ ἤτοι ἀγαθόν γ' ἐστὶν ἢ κακὸν ἢ μεταξὺ τούτων
greek_tokens: e eneka prassw nai ara oun eimi tis o eimi o ou htoi agaqos ge eimi h kakos h metacu outos oute agaqos oute kakos polus anagkh eimi swkraths oukoun legw eimi agaqos men sofia te kai ugieia kai ploutos kai allos o toioutos kakos de enantios outos egw o de mhte agaqos mhte kakos ara toiosde legw o eniote men metexw o agaqos
english_head: Yes. Now is there any existent thing that is not either good or bad or between these—neither good nor bad? Most assuredly nothing, Socrates. Well, do you call wisdom and health and wealth and everything else of that kind good, and their opposites bad? I do. And by things neither good nor bad do you mean such things

greek_head: ἐνίοτε δὲ τοῦ κακοῦ ἐνίοτε δὲ οὐδετέρου οἷον καθῆσθαι καὶ βαδίζειν καὶ τρέχειν καὶ πλεῖν καὶ οἷον αὖ λίθους καὶ ξύλα
greek_tokens: eniote de o kakos eniote de oudeteros oios kaqhmai kai badizw kai trexw kai plew kai oios au liqos kai culon kai allos o toioutos ou outos legw h allos atta kalew o mhte agaqos mhte kakos ou alla outos poteros oun o metacu outos eneka o agaqos prassw otan prassw h agaqos o metacu o
english_head: as sometimes partake of the good, sometimes of the bad, and sometimes of neither—for example, sitting, walking, running, and sailing, or again, stones and sticks and anything else of that sort? These are what you mean, are they not? Or are there other things that you describe as neither good nor bad? No, these are what I mean. Then do people do these intermediate things, when they do them, for the sake of the good things, or the good things for the intermediate? The intermediate, I presume, for

greek_head: μεταξὺ δήπου τῶν ἀγαθῶν Τὸ ἀγαθὸν ἄρα διώκοντες καὶ βαδίζομεν ὅταν βαδίζωμεν οἰόμενοι βέλτιον εἶναι καὶ
greek_tokens: metacu dhpou o agaqos o agaqos ara diwkw kai badizw otan badizw oiomai beltiwn eimi kai o enantion isthmi otan isthmi o autos eneka o agaqos h ou nai oukoun kai apokteinw ei tis apokteinw kai ekballw kai afairew xrhma oiomai ameinwn eimi egw outos poiew h mh panu ge eneka ara o agaqos apas outos poiew e poiew fhmi oukoun omologew o eneka tis poiew mh ekeinos boulomai
english_head: Thus it is in pursuit of the good that we walk, when we walk, conceiving it to be better; or on the contrary, stand, when we stand, for the sake of the same thing, the good: is it not so? Yes. And so we put a man to death, if we do put him to death, or expel him or deprive him of his property, because we think it better for us to do this than not? Certainly. So it is for the sake of the good that the doers of all these things do them? I agree. And we have admitted that when we do things for an o

greek_head: ἀλλ' ἐκεῖνο οὗ ἕνεκα ταῦτα ποιοῦμεν Μάλιστα Οὐκ ἄρα σφάττειν βουλόμεθα οὐδ' ἐκβάλλειν ἐκ τῶν
greek_tokens: alla ekeinos e eneka outos poiew malista ou ara sfazw boulomai oude ekballw ek o polis oude xrhma afairew aplos outws alla ean men wfelimos eimi outos boulomai prassw autos blaberos de eimi ou boulomai o gar agaqos boulomai ws fhmi su o de mhte agaqos mhte kakos ou boulomai oude o kakos eimi gar alhqhs su dokazw legw eimi pwlos h ou tis ou apokrinw alhqhs
english_head: Quite so. Then we do not wish to slaughter people or expel them from our cities or deprive them of their property as an act in itself, but if these things are beneficial we wish to do them, while if they are harmful, we do not wish them. For we wish what is good, as you say; but what is neither good nor bad we do not wish, nor what is bad either, do we? Is what I say true in your opinion, Polus, or not? Why do you not answer? It is true.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν εἴπερ ταῦτα ὁμολογοῦμεν εἴ τις ἀποκτείνει τινὰ ἢ ἐκβάλλει ἐκ πόλεως ἢ ἀφαιρεῖται χρήματα εἴτε
greek_tokens: oukoun eiper outos omologew ei tis apokteinw tis h ekballw ek polis h afairew xrhma eite turannos eimi eite rhtwr oiomai ameinwn eimi autos tugxanw de eimi kakos outos dhpou poiew o dokew autos eimi gar nai ara oun kai o boulomai eiper tugxanw outos kakos eimi tis ou apokrinw alla ou egw dokew poiew o boulomai eimi oun opws o toioutos
english_head: Then, as we agree on this, if a man puts anyone to death or expels him from a city or deprives him of his property, whether he does it as a despot or an orator, because he thinks it better for himself though it is really worse, that man, I take it, does what he thinks fit, does he not? Yes. Now is it also what he wishes, supposing it to be really bad? Why do you not answer? No, I do not think he does what he wishes.

greek_head: μέγα δύναται ἐν τῇ πόλει ταύτῃ εἴπερ ἐστὶ τὸ μέγα δύνασθαι ἀγαθόν τι κατὰ τὴν σὴν ὁμολογίαν Οὐκ
greek_tokens: megas dunamai en o polis outos eiper eimi o megas dunamai agaqos tis kata o shs omologia ou eimi alhqhs ara egw elegos legos ostis eimi anqrwpos poiew en polis o dokew autos mh megas dunamai mhde poiew o boulomai ws dh su eimi swkraths ou an dexomai ecesti su poiew ostis dokew su en o polis mallon h mh oude zhlew otan eidon tis h apokteinw os dokew autos h afairew xrhma h dew dikaios legw h adikos
english_head: Can such a man then be said to have great power in that city, if to have great power is something good, according to your admission? He cannot. Then I spoke the truth when I said that it is possible for a man to do what he thinks fit in a city and yet not to have great power nor to do what he wishes. As if you, Socrates, would not accept the liberty of doing what you think fit in your city rather than not, and would not envy a man whom you observed to have put some one to death as he thought fit

greek_head: Ὁπότερ' ἂν ποιῇ οὐκ ἀμφοτέρως ζηλωτόν ἐστιν Εὐφήμει ὦ Πῶλε
greek_tokens: opoteros an poiew ou amfoteros zhlwtos eimi eufhmew eimi pwlos tis dh ostis ou xrh oute o azhlwtos zhlew oute o aqlios alla eleaw tis de outws su dokew exw peri os egw legos o anqrwpos pws gar ou ostis oun apokteinw os an doca autos dikaios apokteinw aqlios dokew su eimi kai eleeinos ou egw oude mentoi zhlwtos ou arti aqlios fhmi eimi
english_head: Whichever way he does it, is it not enviable in either case? Hush, Polus! Why? Because we ought not to envy either the unenviable or the wretched, but pity them. What! Is that the state in which you consider those people, of whom I speak, to be? Yes, for so I must. Then do you consider that a man who puts another to death as he thinks fit, and justly puts him to death, is wretched and pitiable? Not I; but not enviable either. Did you not say just now that he was wretched?

greek_head: Τὸν ἀδίκως γε ὦ ἑταῖρε ἀποκτείναντα καὶ ἐλεινόν γε πρός τὸν δὲ δικαίως ἀζήλωτον
greek_tokens: o adikos ge eimi etairos apokteinw kai eleeinos ge pros o de dikaios azhlwtos eimi pou o ge apoqnhskw adikos eleeinos te kai aqlios eimi hsswn h o apokteinw eimi pwlos kai hsswn h o dikaios apoqnhskw pws dhta eimi swkraths outws ws megas o kakh tugxanw eimi o adikew eimi gar outos megas ou o adikew megas hkistos ge su ara boulomai an adikew mallon h adikew
english_head: Only he who unjustly put some one to death, my friend, and I called him pitiable as well: if he acted justly, then he is unenviable. I suppose, at any rate, the man who is put to death unjustly is both pitiable and wretched. Less so than he who puts him to death, Polus, and less so than he who is put to death justly. In what way can that be, Socrates ? In this, that to do wrong is the greatest of evils. What, is this the greatest? Is not to suffer wrong a greater? By no means. Then would you wis

greek_head: Βουλοίμην μὲν ἂν ἔγωγε οὐδέτερα εἰ δ' ἀναγκαῖον εἴη ἀδικεῖν ἢ ἀδικεῖσθαι ἑλοίμην ἂν μᾶλλον ἀδικεῖσθαι ἢ ἀδικεῖν
greek_tokens: boulomai men an egw oudeteros ei de anagkaion eaw adikew h adikew airew an mallon adikew h adikew su ara turanneuw ou an dexomai ou ei o turanneuw ge legw osper egw alla egw outos legos osper arti ecesti en o polis o an dokazw autos poiew outos kai apokteinw kai ekballw kai pas prassw kata o autos doca eimi makarios egw dh legw o logos epilambanw
english_head: I should wish neither, for my own part; but if it were necessary either to do wrong or to suffer it, I should choose to suffer rather than do it. Then you would not accept a despot’s power? No, if you mean by a despot’s power the same as I do. Why, what I mean is, as I did just now, the liberty of doing anything one thinks fit in one’s city—putting people to death and expelling them and doing everything at one’s own discretion. My gifted friend, let me speak, and you shall take me to task in you

greek_head: εἰ γὰρ ἐγὼ ἐν ἀγορᾷ πληθούσῃ λαβὼν ὑπὸ μάλης ἐγχειρίδιον λέγοιμι πρὸς σὲ ὅτι Ὦ Πῶλε ἐμοὶ δύναμίς τις καὶ
greek_tokens: ei gar egw en agora plhqw lambanw upo malh egxeiridion legw pros sos ostis eimi pwlos egw dunamis tis kai turannis qaumasios arti prosgignomai ean gar ara egw doca tis outos o anqrwpos os su oraw autika mala dew qnhskw qnhskw outos os an doca kan tis doca egw o kefalh autos katagnumi dew katagnumi eimi autika mala kan qoimation diasxizw diasxizw eimi outws
english_head: Suppose that in a crowded market I should hide a dagger under my arm and then say to you: Polus, I have just acquired, by a wonderful chance, the power of a despot; for if I should think fit that one of those people whom you see there should die this very instant, a dead man he will be, just as I think fit; or if I think fit that one of them shall have his head broken, broken it will be immediately; or to have his cloak torn in pieces,

greek_head: μέγα ἐγὼ δύναμαι ἐν τῇδε τῇ πόλει εἰ οὖν ἀπιστοῦντί σοι δείξαιμι τὸ ἐγχειρίδιον ἴσως ἂν εἴποις ἰδὼν ὅτι Ὦ Σώκρατες
greek_tokens: megas egw dunamai en ode o polis ei oun apistew su deiknumi o egxeiridion isos an eipon eidon ostis eimi swkraths outws men pas an megas dunamai epei kan empimprhmi oikia outos o tropos ostis su dokew kai o ge aqhnaia newrion kai o trihrhs kai o ploion pas kai o dhmosios kai o idios alla ou ara outos eimi o megas dunamai o poiew o dokew autos h dokew su ou dhta outws ge
english_head: torn it will be: so great is my power in this city. Then suppose that on your disbelieving this I showed you my dagger; I expect when you saw it you would say: Socrates, at this rate every one would have great power, for any house you thought fit might be set ablaze on these methods, and the Athenian arsenals also, and the men-of-war and all the rest of the shipping, both public and private. But surely this is not what it is to have great power—merely doing what one thinks fit. Or do you think i

greek_head: Ἔχεις οὖν εἰπεῖν δι' ὅτι μέμφῃ τὴν τοιαύτην δύναμιν
greek_tokens: exis oun eipon dia ostis memfomai o toioutos dunamis egw tis dh legos ostis anagkaion o outws prassw zhmiow eimi o de zhmiow ou kakos panu ge oukoun eimi qaumasios o megas dunamai palin au su fainw ean men prassw o dokew epomai o wfelimos prassw agaqos te eimi kai outos ws eoika eimi o megas dunamai ei de mh kakos kai mikros dunamai
english_head: Then can you tell me why you disapprove of this kind of power? I can. Why, then? Tell me. Because it is inevitable that he who acts thus will be punished. And is it not a bad thing to be punished? Certainly. So, my remarkable friend, you have come round again to the view that if doing what one thinks fit is attended by advantage in doing it, this is not merely a good thing but at the same time, it seems, the possession of great power; otherwise

greek_head: σκεψώμεθα δὲ καὶ τόδε ἄλλο τι ὁμολογοῦμεν ἐνίοτε μὲν ἄμεινον εἶναι ταῦτα ποιεῖν ἃ νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν ἀποκτεινύναι τε
greek_tokens: skeptomai de kai ode allos tis omologew eniote men ameinwn eimi outos poiew o nundi legw apokteinw te kai ecelaunw anqrwpos kai afairew xrhma eniote de ou panu ge outos men dh ws eoika kai para sew kai para egw omologew nai pote oun su fhmi ameinwn eimi outos poiew eipon tis oros orizw su men oun eimi swkraths apokrinw autos outos
english_head: it is a bad thing and means little power. And let us consider another point besides; do we not admit that sometimes it is better to do those things that we were mentioning just now—to put people to death and banish them and deprive them of property—while sometimes it is not? To be sure. Then here is a point, it seems, that is admitted both on your side and on mine. Yes. Then when do you say it is better to do these things? Tell me where you draw the line. Nay, I would rather that you, Socrates,

greek_head: Ἐγὼ μὲν τοίνυν φημί ὦ Πῶλε εἴ σοι παρ' ἐμοῦ ἥδιόν ἐστιν ἀκούειν ὅταν μὲν δικαίως τις ταῦτα ποιῇ
greek_tokens: egw men toinun fhmi eimi pwlos ei su para egw hdus eimi akouw otan men dikaios tis outos poiew ameinwn eimi otan de adikos kakos xalepos ge su elegxw eimi swkraths alla ou kan pas su elegxw ostis ou alhqhs legw polus ara egw o pais xaris exw isos de kai sos ean egw elegxw kai apallassw fluaria alla mh kamnw filos anhr euergeths alla elegxos alla meis eimi swkraths oudeis ge su deomai palaios
english_head: Well then I say, Polus, if you prefer to hear it from me, that it is better when these things are done justly, and worse when unjustly. So hard to refute you, Socrates! Nay, a mere child could do it, could he not, and prove your words are untrue? Then I shall be most grateful to the child, and equally to you, if you refute me and rid me of foolery. Come, do not grow weary in well-doing towards your friend, but refute me.

greek_head: πράγμασιν ἐλέγχειν τὰ γὰρ ἐχθὲς καὶ πρώην γεγονότα ταῦτα ἱκανά σε ἐξελέγξαι ἐστὶν καὶ ἀποδεῖξαι ὡς πολλοὶ ἀδικοῦντες
greek_tokens: pragma elegxw o gar exqes kai prwhn gignomai outos ikanos su ecelegxw eimi kai apodeiknumi ws polus adikew anqrwpos eudaimwn eimi o poios outos arxelaos dhpou outos o perdikkos oraw arxw makedonia ei de mh alla akouw ge eudaimwn oun su dokew eimi h aqlios ou oida eimi pwlos ou gar pw suggignomai o andris
english_head: Well, to be sure, Socrates, there is no need to refute you with ancient instances; for those happenings of but a day or two ago are enough to refute you, and prove that many a wrongdoer is happy. What sort of thing do you mean? I suppose you see that Archelaus, son of Perdiccas, is ruler of Macedonia? Well, if I do not, at any rate I hear it. Do you consider him happy or wretched? I do not know, Polus; I have never met the man.

greek_head: Τί δέ συγγενόμενος ἂν γνοίης ἄλλως δὲ αὐτόθεν οὐ γιγνώσκεις ὅτι εὐδαιμονεῖ
greek_tokens: tis de suggignomai an gignwskw allos de autoqen ou gignwskw ostis eudaimonew ma diw ou dhta dhlos dh eimi swkraths ostis oude o megas basileus gignwskw fhmi eudaimwn eimi kai alhqhs ge eraomai ou gar oida paideia opws exis kai dikaiosunh tis de en outos o pas eudaimonia eimi os ge egw legos eimi pwlos o men gar kalos kai agaqos anhr kai gunh eudaimwn eimi fhmi o de adikos kai ponhros aqlios
english_head: What? Could you find out by meeting him, and cannot otherwise tell, straight off, that he is happy? No, indeed, upon my word. Then doubtless you will say, Socrates, that you do not know that even the Great King is happy. Yes, and I shall be speaking the truth; for I do not know how he stands in point of education and justice. Why, does happiness entirely consist in that? Yes, by my account, Polus; for a good and honorable man or woman, I say, is happy, and an unjust and wicked one is wretched.

greek_head: Ἄθλιος ἄρα οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Ἀρχέλαος κατὰ τὸν σὸν λόγον
greek_tokens: aqlios ara outos eimi o arxelaos kata o sos logos eiper ge eimi filos adikos alla men dh pws ou adikos os ge proshkw men o arxh oudeis os nun exis eimi ek gunh o eimi doulh alketos o perdikkos adelfos kai kata men o dikaios doulos eimi alketos kai ei boulomai o dikaios poiew douleuw an alkeths kai eimi eudaimwn kata o sos logos nun de qaumasios ws aqlios gignomai epei o megas adikew
english_head: Then this Archelaus, on your statement, is wretched? Yes, my friend, supposing he is unjust. Well, but how can he be other than unjust? He had no claim to the throne which he now occupies, being the son of a woman who was a slave of Perdiccas’ brother Alcetas, and in mere justice he was Alcetas’ slave; and if he wished to do what is just, he would be serving Alcetas and would be happy, by your account; but, as it is, he has become a prodigy of wretchedness,

greek_head: ὅς γε πρῶτον μὲν τοῦτον αὐτὸν τὸν δεσπότην καὶ θεῖον μεταπεμψάμενος ὡς ἀποδώσων τὴν ἀρχὴν ἣν Περδίκκας
greek_tokens: os ge proteros men outos autos o despoths kai qew metapempw ws apodidwmi o arxh os perdikkhs autos afairew cenizw kai katamequskw autos te kai o uios autos alecandros aneyios eautou sxedon hlikiwths emballw eis amaca nuktwr ecagw aposfazw te kai afanizw amfoteros kai outos adikew elaunw eautou aqlios gignomai kai ou metamelomai autos alla oligos
english_head: since he has done the most enormous wrong. First of all he invited this very master and uncle of his to his court, as if he were going to restore to him the kingdom of which Perdiccas had deprived him; and after entertaining him and his son Alexander—his own cousin, about the same age as himself—and making them drunk, he packed them into a carriage, drove them away by night, and murdered and made away with them both. And after all these iniquities he failed to observe that he had become a most w

greek_head: ὕστερον τὸν ἀδελφόν τὸν γνήσιον τοῦ Περδίκκου ὑόν παῖδα ὡς ἑπτέτη οὗ ἡ ἀρχὴ ἐγίγνετο κατὰ τὸ δίκαιον οὐκ ἐβουλήθη
greek_tokens: usteron o adelfos o gnhsios o perdikkos uios pais ws epteths e o arxh gignomai kata o dikaios ou boulomai eudaimwn gignomai dikaios ektrefw kai apodidwmi o arxh ekeinos alla eis frear emballw kai apopnigw pros o mhthr autos kleopatrh xhn fhmi diwkw empitnw kai apoqnhskw toigar nun ate megas adikew o en makedonia aqlios eimi pas makedwn alla ou eudaimwn kai isos eimi ostis aqhnaia apo sew
english_head: he refused to make himself happy by bringing up, as he was justly bound, his brother, the legitimate son of Perdiccas, a boy about seven years old who had a just title to the throne, and restoring the kingdom to him; but he cast him into a well and drowned him, and then told his mother Cleopatra that he had fallen in and lost his life while chasing a goose. So now, you see, as the greatest wrongdoer in Macedonia, he is the most wretched of all the Macedonians, not the happiest; and I daresay som

greek_head: ἀρξάμενος δέξαιτ' ἂν ἄλλος ὁστισοῦν Μακεδόνων γενέσθαι μᾶλλον ἢ Ἀρχέλαος
greek_tokens: arxw dexomai an allos ostis makedwn gignomai mallon h arxelaos kai kata arxh o logos eimi pwlos egw su epainew ostis egw dokew eu pros o rhtorikos paideuw o de dialegw amelew kai nun allos tis outos eimi o logos os egw kai an pas ecelegxw kai egw upo sew nun ws su oiomai ecelegxw outos o logos faskw o adikew ou eudaimwn eimi pinw agaqos kai meis oudeis ge su outos omologew os su fhmi
english_head: in preferring to change places with any other Macedonian of them all, rather than with Archelaus! At the beginning of our discussion, Polus, I complimented you on having had, as I consider, a good training in rhetoric, while you seem to have neglected disputation; and now, accordingly, this is the argument, is it, with which any child could refute me? By this statement, you think, I now stand refuted at your hands, when I assert that the wrongdoer is not happy? How so, my good friend? Why, I tel

greek_head: Οὐ γὰρ ἐθέλεις ἐπεὶ δοκεῖ γέ σοι ὡς ἐγὼ λέγω Ὦ μακάριε ῥητορικῶς γάρ με ἐπιχειρεῖς ἐλέγχειν
greek_tokens: ou gar eqelw epei dokew ge su ws egw legos eimi makarios rhtorikos gar egw epixeirew elegxw wsper e en o dikasthrion hgeomai elegxw kai gar ekei e eteros o eteros dokew elegxw epeidan o logos os an legw martus polus parexw kai eudokimos o de enantios legos enos tis parexw h mhdeis outos de o elegxos oudeis acios eimi pros o
english_head: No, because you do not want to; for you really agree with my statement. My gifted friend, that is because you attempt to refute me in rhetorical fashion, as they understand refuting in the law courts. For there, one party is supposed to refute the other when they bring forward a number of reputable witnesses to any statements they may make, whilst their opponent produces only one, or none. But this sort of refutation is quite worthless

greek_head: ἀλήθειαν ἐνίοτε γὰρ ἂν καὶ καταψευδομαρτυρηθείη τις ὑπὸ πολλῶν καὶ δοκούντων εἶναί τι καὶ νῦν περὶ ὧν σὺ λέγεις
greek_tokens: alhqeia eniote gar an kai katayeudomarturew tis upo polus kai dokew eimi tis kai nun peri os su legw oligos su pas sumfhmi tautos aqhnaios kai e cenos ean boulomai kata egw martus parexw ws ou alhqhs legos marturew su ean men boulomai nikias o nikhratos kai e adelfos meta autos os e tripous e efechs isthmi eimi en o dionusion ean de boulomai aristokraths
english_head: for getting at the truth; since occasionally a man may actually be crushed by the number and reputation of the false witnesses brought against him. And so now you will find almost everybody, Athenians and foreigners, in agreement with you on the points you state, if you like to bring forward witnesses against the truth of what I say: if you like, there is Nicias, son of Niceratus, with his brothers, whose tripods are standing in a row in the Dionysium; or else Aristocrates, son of Scellias, whos

greek_head: ὁ Σκελλίου οὗ αὖ ἐστιν ἐν Πυθίου τοῦτο τὸ καλὸν ἀνάθημα ἐὰν δὲ βούλῃ ἡ Περικλέους ὅλη οἰκία ἢ ἄλλη συγγένεια
greek_tokens: o skelliou e au eimi en puqion outos o kalos anaqhma ean de boulomai o periklehs oloc oikia h allos suggeneia ostis an boulomai o enqade eklegw alla egw su eis eimi ou omologew ou gar egw su anagkazw alla yeudomartus polus kata egw parexw epixeirew ekballw egw ek o ousia kai o alhqhs egw de an mh sos autos enos eimi martus parexw omologew peri os legos oudeis oimh acios logos egw perainw peri os an
english_head: or if you choose, there is the whole house of Pericles or any other family you may like to select in this place. But I, alone here before you, do not admit it, for you fail to convince me: you only attempt, by producing a number of false witnesses against me, to oust me from my reality, the truth. But if on my part I fail to produce yourself as my one witness to confirm what I say, I consider I have achieved nothing of any account

greek_head: ἡμῖν ὁ λόγος ᾖ οἶμαι δὲ οὐδὲ σοί ἐὰν μὴ ἐγώ σοι μαρτυρῶ εἷς ὢν μόνος τοὺς δ' ἄλλους πάντας τούτους χαίρειν ἐᾷς ἔστιν
greek_tokens: egw o logos eimi oimh de oude sos ean mh egw su marturomai eis eimi monos o de allos pas outos xairw eaw eimi men oun outos tis tropos elegxos ws su te oiomai kai allos polus eimi de kai allos os egw au oimh paraballw oun para allhlwn skeptomai ei tis diaferw allhlwn kai gar kai tugxanw peri os amfisbhtew ou panu mikros eimi alla sxedon tis outos peri os oida te kalos mh oida te aisxros o gar kefalaios autos eimi h gignwskw h agnoew ostis te eudaimwn eimi kai
english_head: towards the matter of our discussion, whatever it may be; nor have you either, I conceive, unless I act alone as your one witness, and you have nothing to do with all these others. Well now, this is one mode of refutation, as you and many other people understand it; but there is also another which I on my side understand. Let us therefore compare them with each other and consider if there is a difference between them. For indeed the points which we have at issue are by no means of slight importa

greek_head: ὅστις μή αὐτίκα πρῶτον περὶ οὗ νῦν ὁ λόγος ἐστίν σὺ ἡγῇ οἷόν τε εἶναι μακάριον ἄνδρα ἀδικοῦντά τε καὶ ἄδικον
greek_tokens: ostis mh autika proteros peri e nun o logos eimi su hgeomai oios te eimi makarios anhr adikew te kai adikos eimi eiper arxelaos adikos men hgeomai eimi eudaimwn de allos tis ws outws su nomizw dianaw panu ge egw de fhmi adunatos eis men outos amfisbhtew eien adikew de dh eudaimwn eimi ara an tugxanw dikh te kai timwria hkistos ge epei outws ge an aqlios eaw
english_head: with the point we are now debating, you consider it possible for a man to be happy while doing wrong, and as a wrongdoer, since you regard Archelaus as a wrongdoer, and yet happy. We are to conclude, are we not, that this is your opinion? Certainly. And I say it is impossible. There we have one point at issue. Very good but then, will a man be happy in wrongdoing if he comes in for requital and punishment? Not at all, since in that case he would be most wretched.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἐὰν ἄρα μὴ τυγχάνῃ δίκης ὁ ἀδικῶν κατὰ τὸν σὸν λόγον εὐδαίμων ἔσται
greek_tokens: alla ean ara mh tugxanw dikh o adikew kata o sos logos eudaimwn eimi fhmi kata de ge o emos doca eimi pwlos o adikew te kai o adikos pantws men aqlios aqlios mentoi ean mh didwmi dikh mhde tugxanw timwria adikew hsswn de aqlios ean didwmi dikh kai tugxanw dikh upo qea te kai anqrwpos
english_head: But if the wrongdoer escapes requital, by your account he will be happy? Yes. Whereas in my opinion, Polus, the wrongdoer or the unjust is wretched anyhow; more wretched, however, if he does not pay the penalty and gets no punishment for his wrongdoing, but less wretched if he pays the penalty and meets with requital from gods and men.

greek_head: Ἄτοπά γε ὦ Σώκρατες ἐπιχειρεῖς λέγειν Πειράσομαι δέ γε καὶ σὲ ποιῆσαι ὦ ἑταῖρε ταὐτὰ
greek_tokens: atopos ge eimi swkraths epixeirew legw peiraw de ge kai sos poiew eimi etairos tautos egw legw filos gar su hgeomai nun men oun o diaferw outos eimi skopaw de kai su eipon egw pou en o emprosqen o adikew o adikew kakos eimi panu ge su de o adikew nai kai o adikew aqlios fhmi eimi egw kai ecelegxw upo sew nai ma dios
english_head: What a strange doctrine, Socrates, you are trying to maintain! Yes, and I will endeavor to make you too, my friend, maintain it with me: for I count you as a friend. Well now, these are the points on which we differ; just examine them yourself. I think I told you at an earlier stage that wrongdoing was worse than being wronged. Certainly you did. And you thought that being wronged was worse. Yes. And I said that wrongdoers were wretched, and I was refuted by you. Upon my word, yes.

greek_head: Ὡς σύ γε οἴει ὦ Πῶλε Ἀληθῆ γε οἰόμενος
greek_tokens: ws su ge oiomai eimi pwlos alhqhs ge oiomai isos su de ge eudaimwn au o adikew ean mh didhmi dikh panu men oun egw de ge autos aqlios fhmi o de didwmi dikh hsswn boulomai kai outos elegxw alla eti outos ekeinos xalepos eimi eimi swkraths ecelegxw ou dhta eimi pwlos alla adunatos o gar alhqhs oudepote elegxw pws legw ean adikew anqrwpos lambanw turannis
english_head: At least to your thinking, Polus. Yes, and true thinking too. Perhaps. But you said, on the other hand, that wrongdoers are happy, if they pay no penalty. Certainly. Whereas I say they are most wretched, and those who pay the penalty, less so. Do you wish to refute that as well? Why, that is still harder to refute, Socrates, than the other! Not merely so, Polus, but impossible; for the truth is never refuted.

greek_head: ἐπιβουλεύων καὶ ληφθεὶς στρεβλῶται καὶ ἐκτέμνηται καὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ἐκκάηται καὶ ἄλλας πολλὰς καὶ μεγάλας
greek_tokens: epibouleuw kai lambanw streblow kai ektemnw kai o ofqalmos ekkaiw kai allos polus kai megas kai pantodapos lwbh autos te lwbaomai kai o eautou epeidon pais te kai gunh o esxatos anastaurow h katapissow outos eudaimwn eimi h ean diafeugw turannos kaqisthmi kai arxw en o polis diabiazomai poa ostis an boulomai zhlwtos eimi kai eudaimonizw upo o
english_head: How do you mean? If a man be caught criminally plotting to make himself a despot, and he be straightway put on the rack and castrated and have his eyes burnt out, and after suffering himself, and seeing inflicted on his wife and children, a number of grievous torments of every kind, he be finally crucified or burnt in a coat of pitch, will he be happier than if he escape and make himself despot, and pass his life as the ruler in his city, doing whatever he likes, and envied and congratulated by

greek_head: πολιτῶν καὶ τῶν ἄλλων ξένων ταῦτα λέγεις ἀδύνατον εἶναι ἐξελέγχειν
greek_tokens: poliths kai o allos cenos outos legw adunatos eimi ecelegxw mormoluttomai au eimi gennaios pwlos kai ou elegxw arti de marturomai omws de upomimnhskw egw mikros ean adikos epibouleuw turannis eipon egw eudaimwn men toinun oudepote eimi oudeteros autos oute o katergazomai o turannis adikos oute o didwmi dikh duo gar aqlios eudaimwn men
english_head: Impossible, do you tell me, to refute that? You are trying to make my flesh creep this time, my spirited Polus, instead of refuting me; a moment ago you were for calling witnesses. However, please refresh my memory a little: criminally plotting to make himself a despot, you said? I did. Then neither of them will ever be happier than the other—neither he who has unjustly compassed the despotic power, nor he who pays the penalty; for of two wretched persons

greek_head: οὐκ ἂν εἴη ἀθλιώτερος μέντοι ὁ διαφεύγων καὶ τυραννεύσας τί τοῦτο ὦ Πῶλε γελᾷς ἄλλο αὖ τοῦτο εἶδος ἐλέγχου
greek_tokens: ou an eaw aqlios mentoi o diafeugw kai turanneuw tis outos eimi pwlos gelaw allos au outos eidos elegxos eimi epeidan tis tis eipon katagelaw elegxw de mh ou oiomai ecelegxw eimi swkraths otan toioutos legos o oudeis an fhmi anqrwpos epei eromai tis outos eimi pwlos ou eimi o politikos kai perusi bouleuw lagxanw epei o fulh prutaneuw kai dew egw epiyhfizw
english_head: neither can be happier; but still more wretched is he who goes scot-free and establishes himself as despot. What is that I see, Polus? You are laughing? Here we have yet another form of refutation—when a statement is made, to laugh it down, instead of disproving it! Do you not think yourself utterly refuted, Socrates, when you make such statements as nobody in the world would assent to? You have only to ask anyone of the company here. Polus, I am not one of your statesmen: indeed, last year, whe

greek_head: γέλωτα παρεῖχον καὶ οὐκ ἠπιστάμην ἐπιψηφίζειν μὴ οὖν μηδὲ νῦν με κέλευε ἐπιψηφίζειν τοὺς παρόντας ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: gelws parexw kai ou epistamai epiyhfizw mh oun mhde nun egw keleuw epiyhfizw o pareimi alla ei mh exis outos beltiwn elegxos osper nundi egw elegos egw en o meros paradidwmi kai peirw o elegxos oios egw oimh dew eimi egw gar os an legos enos men parexw martus efisthmi autos pros os an egw o logos eimi o de polus eaw xairw kai enos epiyhfizw efisthmi o de
english_head: I had to put a question to the vote, I got laughed at for not understanding the procedure. So do not call upon me again to take the votes of the company now; but if, as I said this moment, you have no better disproof than those, hand the work over to me in my turn, and try the sort of refutation that I think the case requires. For I know how to produce one witness in support of my statements, and that is the man himself with whom I find myself arguing; the many I dismiss: there is also one whose

greek_head: πολλοῖς οὐδὲ διαλέγομαι ὅρα οὖν εἰ ἐθελήσεις ἐν τῷ μέρει διδόναι ἔλεγχον ἀποκρινόμενος τὰ ἐρωτώμενα ἐγὼ γὰρ δὴ
greek_tokens: polus oude dialegw oraw oun ei eqelw en o meros didwmi elegxos apokrinw o erwtaw egw gar dh oimh kai egw kai sos kai o allos anqrwpos o adikew o adikew kakos hgeomai kai o mh didwmi dikh o didwmi egw de ge oute egw oute allos anqrwpos oudeis epei su dexomai an mallon adikew h adikew kai su ge an kai e allos pas polus ge deomai alla oute egw oute su oute allos oudeis
english_head: See therefore if you will consent to be put to the proof in your turn by answering my questions. For I think, indeed, that you and I and the rest of the world believe that doing wrong is worse than suffering it, and escaping punishment worse than incurring it. And I, that neither I nor anyone else in the world believes it. You, it seems, would choose rather to suffer wrong than to do it. Yes, and so would you and everyone else. Far from it neither; I nor you nor anybody else.

greek_head: Οὔκουν ἀποκρινῇ Πάνυ μὲν οὖν καὶ γὰρ ἐπιθυμῶ εἰδέναι ὅτι ποτ'
greek_tokens: oukoun apokrinw panu men oun kai gar epiqumew oida ostis pote erew legos dh egw ina oida wsper an ei ek arxh su erwtaw poteros dokew su eimi pwlos kakos eimi o adikew h o adikew o adikew egw tis de dh aisxros poteros o adikew h o adikew apokrinw o adikew oukoun kai kakos eiper aisxros hkistos ge manqanw ou
english_head: Then will you answer? To be sure I will, for indeed I am eager to know what on earth you will say. Well then, so that you may know, tell me, just as though I were asking you all over again, which of the two seems to you, Polus, to be the worse—doing wrong or suffering it? Suffering it, I say. Now again, which is fouler—doing wrong or suffering it? Answer. Doing it. And also more evil, if fouler. Not at all. I see: you hold, apparently, that fair

greek_head: ταὐτὸν ἡγῇ σύ ὡς ἔοικας καλόν τε καὶ ἀγαθὸν καὶ κακὸν καὶ αἰσχρόν Οὐ δῆτα
greek_tokens: tautos hgeomai su ws eoika kalos te kai agaqos kai kakos kai aisxros ou dhta tis de ode o kalos pas oios kai swma kai xrwma kai sxhma kai fwnh kai epithdeuma eis oudeis apoblepw kalew ekastote kalos oios proteros o swma o kalos ou htoi kata o xreia legw kalos eimi pros o an ekastos xrhsimos eimi pros outos h kata hdona tis ean en o qewrew xairw poiew o qewrew exis tis ektos outos legw peri swma kallos
english_head: and good are not the same, nor evil and foul. Just so. But what of this? All fair things, like bodies and colors and figures and sounds and observances—is it according to no standard that you call these fair in each case? Thus in the first place, when you say that fair bodies are fair, it must be either in view of their use for some particular purpose that each may serve, or in respect of some pleasure arising when, in the act of beholding them, they cause delight to the beholder. Have you any d

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔχω Οὐκοῦν καὶ τἆλλα πάντα οὕτω καὶ σχήματα καὶ χρώματα ἢ διὰ ἡδονήν τινα ἢ διὰ
greek_tokens: ou exw oukoun kai allos pas outws kai sxhma kai xrwma h dia hdona tis h dia wfeleia h dia amfoteros kalos prosagoreuw egw ou kai o fwnh kai o kata o mousikh pas wsautws nai kai meis o ge kata o nomos kai o epithdeuma ou dhpou ektos outos eimi o kalos o h wfelimos eimi h hdus h amfoteros
english_head: of bodily beauty? I have not. And so with all the rest in the same way, whether they be figures or colors, is it for some pleasure or benefit or both that you give them the name of fair? It is. And sounds also, and the effects of music, are not these all in the same case? Yes. And further, in all that belongs to laws and observances, surely the fairness of them cannot lie beyond those limits of being either beneficial or pleasant or both. I think not.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ Οὐκοῦν καὶ τὸ τῶν μαθημάτων κάλλος ὡσαύτως Πάνυ γε καὶ καλῶς γε
greek_tokens: ou egw dokew oukoun kai o o maqhma kallos wsautws panu ge kai kalos ge nun orizw eimi swkraths hdona te kai agaqos orizw o kalos oukoun o aisxros o enantios luph te kai kakazw anagkh otan ara duo kalos eteros kallion eimi h o eteros outos h amfoteros uperballw kallion eimi htoi hdona h wfeleia h amfoteros panu ge kai otan de dh duo aisxros o
english_head: And is it not just the same with the fairness of studies also? Doubtless; and this time, Socrates, your definition is quite fair, when you define what is fair by pleasure and good. And foul by their opposites, pain and evil? That needs must follow. Thus when of two fair things one is fairer, the cause is that it surpasses in either one or both of these effects, either in pleasure, or in benefit, or in both. Certainly. And again, when one of two foul things is fouler,

greek_head: ἕτερον αἴσχιον ᾖ ἤτοι λύπῃ ἢ κακῷ ὑπερβάλλον αἴσχιον ἔσται ἢ οὐκ ἀνάγκη Ναί
greek_tokens: eteros aisxros eimi htoi luph h kakazw uperballw aisxros eimi h ou anagkh nai ferw dh pws legw nundi peri o adikew kai adikew ou legw o men adikew kakos eimi o de adikew aisxros elegos oukoun eiper aisxros o adikew o adikew htoi luphros eimi kai luph uperballw aisxros an eaw h kakazw h amfoteros ou kai outos anagkh pws gar ou proteros
english_head: this will be due to an excess either of pain or of evil: must not that be so? Yes. Come then, what was it we heard just now about doing and suffering wrong? Were you not saying that suffering wrong is more evil, but doing it fouler? I was. Well now, if doing wrong is fouler than suffering it, it is either more painful, and fouler by an excess of pain or evil or both; must not this also be the case? Yes, of course. Then let us first consider if doing wrong

greek_head: μὲν δὴ σκεψώμεθα ἆρα λύπῃ ὑπερβάλλει τὸ ἀδικεῖν τοῦ ἀδικεῖσθαι καὶ ἀλγοῦσι μᾶλλον οἱ ἀδικοῦντες ἢ οἱ ἀδικούμενοι
greek_tokens: men dh skeptomai ara luph uperballw o adikew o adikew kai algew mallon e adikew h e adikew oudamos eimi swkraths outos ge ou ara luph ge uperexw ou dhta oukoun ei mh luph amfoteros men ou an eti uperballw ou fainw oukoun o eteros leipw nai o kakazw eoika oukoun kakazw uperballw o adikew kakos an eaw o adikew dhlos dh ostis
english_head: exceeds suffering it in point of pain—if those who do wrong are more pained than those who suffer it. Not so at all, Socrates. Then it does not surpass in pain. No, indeed. And so, if not in pain, it can no longer be said to exceed in both. Apparently. It remains, then, that it exceeds in the other. Yes. In evil. So it seems. Then it is by an excess of evil that doing wrong is fouler than suffering it. Yes, obviously.

greek_head: Ἄλλο τι οὖν ὑπὸ μὲν τῶν πολλῶν ἀνθρώπων καὶ ὑπὸ σοῦ ὡμολογεῖτο ἡμῖν ἐν τῷ ἔμπροσθεν χρόνῳ αἴσχιον
greek_tokens: allos tis oun upo men o polus anqrwpos kai upo sew omologew egw en o emprosqen xronos aisxros eimi o adikew o adikew nai nun de ge kakos fainw eoika dexomai an oun su mallon o kakos kai o aisxros anti o hsswn mh oknew apokrinw eimi pwlos oudeis gar blaptw alla gennaios o logos wsper iatros paraxwnnumi apokrinw kai h
english_head: Now it is surely admitted by the mass of mankind, as it was too by you in our talk a while ago, that doing wrong is fouler than suffering it. Yes. And now it has been found to be more evil. So it seems. Then would you rather have the evil and foul when it is more than when it is less? Do not shrink from answering, Polus you will get no hurt by it: but submit yourself bravely to the argument, as to a doctor, and reply yes or no to my question.

greek_head: φάθι ἢ μὴ ἃ ἐρωτῶ Ἀλλ' οὐκ ἂν δεξαίμην ὦ Σώκρατες Ἄλλος δέ τις ἀνθρώπων Οὔ μοι
greek_tokens: fhmi h mh o erwtaw alla ou an dexomai eimi swkraths allos de tis anqrwpos ou egw dokew kata ge outos o logos alhqhs ara egw elegos ostis oute an egw oute an su oute allos oudeis anqrwpos dexomai an mallon adikew h adikew kakos gar tugxanw eimi fainw oraw oun eimi pwlos o elegxos para o elegxos paraballw ostis oudeis eoika alla sos men e allos pas omologew plhn egw egw de su ecarkew eis eimi
english_head: Why, I should not so choose, Socrates. And would anybody else in the world? I think not, by this argument at least. Then I spoke the truth when I said that neither you nor anyone else in the world would choose to do wrong rather than suffer it, since it really is more evil. Apparently. So you see, Polus, that when one proof is contrasted with the other they have no resemblance, but whereas you have the assent of every one else except myself, I am satisfied with your sole and single assent and ev

greek_head: μόνος καὶ ὁμολογῶν καὶ μαρτυρῶν καὶ ἐγὼ σὲ μόνον ἐπιψηφίζων τοὺς ἄλλους ἐῶ χαίρειν καὶ τοῦτο μὲν ἡμῖν οὕτως
greek_tokens: monos kai omologew kai marturew kai egw sos monos epiyhfizw o allos eaw xairw kai outos men egw outws exw meta outos de peri e o deuteros amfiamfisbhtew skeptomai o adikew didwmi dikh ara megas o kakh eimi ws su oiomai h megas o mh didwmi ws au egw oiomai skopaw de ode o didwmi dikh kai o kolazw dikaios adikew ara o autos kalew egw
english_head: and I take but your vote only and disregard the rest. Now let us leave this matter where it stands, and proceed next to examine the second part on which we found ourselves at issue—whether for a wrongdoer to pay the penalty is the greatest of evils, as you supposed, or to escape it is a greater, as I on my side held. Let us look at it this way: do you call paying the just penalty, and being justly punished, for wrongdoing the same thing? I do.

greek_head: Ἔχεις οὖν λέγειν ὡς οὐχὶ τά γε δίκαια πάντα καλά ἐστιν καθ' ὅσον δίκαια καὶ διασκεψάμενος εἰπέ
greek_tokens: exis oun legw ws ou o ge dikaios pas kalos eimi kaqa osos dikaios kai diaskeptomai eipon alla egw dokew eimi swkraths skopaw dh kai ode ara ei tis tis poiew anagkh tis eimi kai pasxw upo outos o poiew egw dokew ara outos pasxw o o poiew poiew kai toioutos oios poiew o poiew legos de o toiosde ei tis tuptw anagkh tis tuptw anagkh kai ei sfodra tuptw h taxus o
english_head: And can you maintain that all just things are not fair, in so far as they are just? Consider well before you speak. No, I think they are, Socrates. Then take another point: if a man does anything, must there be something which is also acted upon by this doer of the thing? I think so. And does it suffer what the doer does, and is the effect such as the agent’s action makes it? I mean, for example, when one strikes a blow something must needs be struck? It must. And if the striker strikes hard or

greek_head: τύπτων οὕτω καὶ τὸ τυπτόμενον τύπτεσθαι Ναί Τοιοῦτον ἄρα πάθος τῷ τυπτομένῳ ἐστὶν οἷον ἂν τὸ
greek_tokens: tuptw outws kai o tuptw tuptw nai toioutos ara paqos o tuptw eimi oios an o tuptw poiew panu ge oukoun kai ei kaos tis anagkh tis kaiw pws gar ou kai ei sfodra ge kaos h algeinos outws kaiw o kaiw ws an o kaiw kaiw panu ge oukoun kai ei temnw tis o autos logos temnw gar tis nai kai ei megas ge h baqus o tmhma h algeinos toioutos
english_head: the thing struck is struck in the same way? Yes. Hence the effect in the thing struck is such as the striker makes it? Certainly. And so again, if one burns, something must be burnt? Yes, of course. And if one burns severely or sorely, the thing burnt is burnt according as the burner burns it? Certainly. And again, if one cuts, the same may be said? For something is cut. Yes. And if the cut is large or deep or sore,

greek_head: τμῆμα τέμνεται τὸ τεμνόμενον οἷον τὸ τέμνον τέμνει Φαίνεται Συλλήβδην δὴ ὅρα εἰ ὁμολογεῖς ὃ
greek_tokens: tmhma temnw o temnw oios o temnw temnw fainw sullhbdhn dh oraw ei omologew o arti elegos peri pas oios an poiew o poiew toioutos o pasxw pasxw alla omologew outos dh omologew o dikh didwmi poteros pasxw tis eimi h poiew anagkh eimi swkraths pasxw oukoun upo tis poiew pws gar ou upo ge o kolazw o de orqos kolazw
english_head: the cut made in the thing cut is such as the cutter cuts it? Apparently. Then putting it all in a word, see if you agree that what I was just saying applies to all cases—that the patient receives an effect of the same kind as the agent’s action. I do agree. Then this being admitted, is paying the penalty suffering something, or doing it? Suffering it must be, Socrates. And at the hands of an agent? Yes, of course; at the hands of the punisher.

greek_head: δικαίως κολάζει Ναί Δίκαια ποιῶν ἢ οὔ Δίκαια Οὐκοῦν ὁ κολαζόμενος δίκην διδοὺς
greek_tokens: dikaios kolazw nai dikaios poa h ou dikaios oukoun o kolazw dikh didwmi dikaios pasxw fainw o de dikaios pou kalos omologew panu ge outos ara o men poiew kalos o de pasxw o kolazw nai
english_head: And he who punishes aright punishes justly? Yes. Doing what is just, or not? What is just. And he who pays the penalty by being punished suffers what is just? Apparently. And what is just, I think we have agreed, is fair? Certainly. Then of these two, the one does what is fair and the other, he who is punished, suffers it. Yes.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν εἴπερ καλά ἀγαθά ἢ γὰρ ἡδέα ἢ ὠφέλιμα Ἀνάγκη Ἀγαθὰ ἄρα πάσχει ὁ δίκην
greek_tokens: oukoun eiper kalos agaqos h gar hdus h wfelimos anagkh agaqos ara pasxw o dikh didwmi eoika wfelew ara nai ara os egw upolambanw o wfeleia beltiwn o yuxh gignomai eiper dikaios kolazw eoika ge kakia ara yuxazw apallassw o dikh didwmi nai ara oun o megas apallassw
english_head: And so, if fair, good? For that is either pleasant or beneficial. It must be so. So he who pays the penalty suffers what is good? It seems so. Then he is benefited? Yes. Is it the benefit I imagine—that he becomes better in soul if he is justly punished? Quite likely. Then is he who pays the penalty relieved from badness of soul? Yes. And so relieved from the greatest evil?

greek_head: κακοῦ ὧδε δὲ σκόπει ἐν χρημάτων κατασκευῇ ἀνθρώπου κακίαν ἄλλην τινὰ ἐνορᾷς ἢ πενίαν Οὔκ
greek_tokens: kakos wde de skopaw en xrhma kataskeuazw anqrwpos kakia allos tis enoraw h penia ou alla penia tis de en swma kataskeuazw kakia an fhmi asqeneia eimi kai nosos kai aisxos kai o toioutos egw oukoun kai en yuxw ponhria hgeomai tis eimi pws gar ou outos oun ou adikia kalew kai amaqia kai deilia kai o toioutos panu men oun oukoun xrhma
english_head: Look at it this way; in a man’s pecuniary resources do you perceive any other badness than poverty? No, only poverty. And what in his bodily resources? You would say that badness there is weakness or disease or ugliness or the like? I would. And in soul too you believe there is a certain wickedness? Of course. And do you not call this injustice, ignorance, cowardice, and so forth? Certainly I do.

greek_head: καὶ σώματος καὶ ψυχῆς τριῶν ὄντων τριττὰς εἴρηκας πονηρίας πενίαν νόσον ἀδικίαν Ναί Τίς οὖν
greek_tokens: kai swma kai yuxazw treis eimi trissos erw ponhria penia nosos adikia nai tis oun outos o ponhria aisxros ou o adikia kai sullhbdhn o o yuxazw ponhria polus ge ei dh aisxros kai kakos pws eimi swkraths legw wdi aei o aisxros htoi luph megas parexw h blabh h amfoteros aisxros eimi ek o omologew en o emprosqen malista aisxros de adikia kai sumpas yuxazw ponhria nundi
english_head: So now in property, body, and soul, these three, you have mentioned three vices—poverty, disease, and injustice? Yes. Then which of these vices is the foulest? Is it not injustice—in short, the vice of the soul? Far the foulest. And if foulest, then also most evil? How do you mean, Socrates? Just this: the foulest is foulest in each case because it produces the greatest pain or harm or both; this follows from our previous admissions. Quite so. And foulest of all, we have just agreed, is injustic

greek_head: ὡμολόγηται ἡμῖν Ὡμολόγηται γάρ Οὐκοῦν ἢ ἀνιαρότατόν ἐστι καὶ ἀνίᾳ ὑπερβάλλον αἴσχιστον τούτων
greek_tokens: omologew egw omologew gar oukoun h aniaros eimi kai ania uperballw aisxros outos eimi h blabh h amfoteros anagkh ara oun algeinos eimi o penomai kai kamnw o adikos eimi kai akolastos kai deilos kai amaqhs ou egw dokew eimi swkraths apo outos ge uperfuomai tis ara ws megas blabh kai kakazw qaumasios uperballw
english_head: in general, vice of soul? Yes, we have. So then either it is most painful, that is, foulest of these vices by an excess of painfulness, or else of harmfulness, or in both ways? Necessarily. Then do you think that being unjust, licentious, cowardly, and ignorant is more painful than being poor and sick? No, I do not, Socrates, from what we have said. Portentous then must be the extent of harm, and astonishing the evil, by which the soul’s vice exceeds all the others

greek_head: τἆλλα ἡ τῆς ψυχῆς πονηρία αἴσχιστόν ἐστι πάντων ἐπειδὴ οὐκ ἀλγηδόνι γε ὡς ὁ σὸς λόγος Φαίνεται
greek_tokens: allos o o yuxazw ponhria aisxros eimi pas epei ou alghdwn ge ws o sos logos fainw alla meis pou o ge megas blabh uperballw megas an kakos eaw o eimi nai o adikia ara kai o akolasia kai o allos yuxazw ponhria megas o eimi kakos eimi fainw tis oun texnh penia apallassw ou xrhmatistikos nai tis de nosos ou iatrikos
english_head: so as to be foulest of all, since it is not by pain, on your view of the matter. Apparently. But further, I suppose, whatever has an excess of harm in the greatest measure, must be the greatest evil in the world. Yes. So injustice, licentiousness, and in general, vice of soul, are the greatest evils in the world? Apparently. Now what is the art that relieves from poverty? Is it not money-making? Yes. And what from disease? Is it not medicine? It must be.

greek_head: Ἀνάγκη Τίς δὲ πονηρίας καὶ ἀδικίας εἰ μὴ οὕτως εὐπορεῖς ὧδε σκόπει ποῖ ἄγομεν καὶ παρὰ τίνας
greek_tokens: anagkh tis de ponhria kai adikia ei mh outws euporew wde skopaw poi agw kai para tis o kamnw o swma para o iatros eimi swkraths poi de o adikew kai o akolastainw para o dikasths legw oukoun dikh didwmi fhmi ara oun ou dikaiosunh tis xraomai kolazw e orqos kolazw dhlos dh xrhmatistikos men ara penia apallassw
english_head: And what from wickedness and injustice? If you are not ready for that offhand, consider it thus: whither and to whom do we take those who are in bodily sickness? To the doctor, Socrates. And whither the wrongdoers and libertines? To the law-court, do you mean? Yes, and to pay the penalty? I agree. Then is it not by employing a kind of justice that those punish who punish aright? Clearly so. Then money-making relieves us from poverty,

greek_head: ἰατρικὴ δὲ νόσου δίκη δὲ ἀκολασίας καὶ ἀδικίας Φαίνεται
greek_tokens: iatrikos de nosos dikh de akolasia kai adikia fainw tis oun outos kalos eimi os legw tinw legw xrhmatistikos iatrikos dikh polus diaferw eimi swkraths o dikh oukoun au htoi hdona pleistos poiew h wfeleia h amfoteros eiper kalos eimi nai ara oun o iatreuw hdus eimi kai xairw e iatreuw ou egw dokew alla wfelimos ge eimi gar
english_head: medicine from disease, and justice from licentiousness and injustice. Apparently. Which then is the fairest of these things? Of what things, pray? Moneymaking, medicine, justice. Justice, Socrates, is far above the others. Now again, if it is fairest, it causes either most pleasure or benefit or both. Yes. Well then, is it pleasant to be medically treated, and do those who undergo such treatment enjoy it? I do not think so. But it is beneficial, is it not?

greek_head: Ναί Μεγάλου γὰρ κακοῦ ἀπαλλάττεται ὥστε λυσιτελεῖ ὑπομεῖναι τὴν ἀλγηδόνα καὶ ὑγιῆ εἶναι Πῶς
greek_tokens: nai megas gar kakos apallassw wste lusitelew upomenw o alghdwn kai ugihs eimi pws gar ou ara oun outws an peri swma eudaimwn anqrwpos eaw iatreuw h mhde kamnw arxh dhlos ostis mhde kamnw ou gar outos eimi eudaimonia ws eoika kakos apallagh alla o arxh mhde kthsis eimi outos
english_head: Yes. Because one is relieved of a great evil, and hence it is worth while to endure the pain and be well. Of course. Is this then the happiest state of body for a man to be in—that of being medically treated—or that of never being ill at all? Clearly, never being ill. Yes, for what we regarded as happiness, it seems, was not this relief from evil, but its non-acquisition at any time. That is so.

greek_head: Τί δέ ἀθλιώτερος πότερος δυοῖν ἐχόντοιν κακὸν εἴτ' ἐν σώματι εἴτ' ἐν ψυχῇ ὁ ἰατρευόμενος καὶ ἀπαλλαττόμενος
greek_tokens: tis de aqlios poteros duo exw kakos eite en swma eite en yuxw o iatreuw kai apallassw o kakos h o mh iatreuw exw de fainw egw o mh iatreuw oukoun o dikh didwmi megas kakos apallagh eimi ponhria eimi gar swfronizw gar pou kai dikaios poiew kai iatrikos gignomai ponhria o dikh nai eudaimwn men ara o mh exw kakia en yuxw epei outos
english_head: Well now, which is the more wretched of two persons who have something evil either in body or in soul, he who is medically treated and is relieved of the evil, or he who is not treated and keeps it? To my thinking, he who is not treated. And we found that paying the penalty is a relief from the greatest evil, wickedness? We did. Because, I suppose, the justice of the court reforms us and makes us juster, and acts as a medicine for wickedness. Yes.

greek_head: μέγιστον τῶν κακῶν ἐφάνη Δῆλον δή Δεύτερος δέ που ὁ ἀπαλλαττόμενος Ἔοικεν Οὗτος
greek_tokens: megas o kakh fainw dhlos dh deuteros de pou o apallassw eoika outos de eimi o nouqetew te kai epiplhssw kai dikh didwmi nai kakos ara zaw o exw adikia kai mh apallassw fainw oukoun outos tugxanw eimi os an o megas adikew kai xraomai megas adikia diaprassw wste mhte nouqetew
english_head: Happiest therefore is he who has no vice in his soul, since we found this to be the greatest of evils. Clearly so. Next after him, I take it, is he who is relieved of it. So it seems. And that was the man who is reproved, reprimanded, and made to pay the penalty. Yes. Hence the worst life is led by him who has the vice and is not relieved of it. Apparently. And this is the man who in committing the greatest wrongs and practicing the greatest injustice has contrived to escape reproof and chastise

greek_head: μήτε κολάζεσθαι μήτε δίκην διδόναι ὥσπερ σὺ φῂς Ἀρχέλαον παρεσκευάσθαι καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους τυράννους
greek_tokens: mhte kolazw mhte dikh didwmi wsper su fhmi arxelaos paraskeuazw kai o allos turannos kai rhtwr kai dunasths eoika sxedon gar pou outos eimi aristos o autos diaprassw eimi wsper an ei tis o megas noshma sunisxw diaprassw mh didwmi dikh o peri o swma amarthma o iatros mhde iatreuw fobew wsper pas o kaiw kai o temnw ostis
english_head: as you say Archelaus has succeeded in doing, and the rest of the despots and orators and overlords? So it seems. Because, I conceive, my excellent friend, what these persons have contrived for themselves is very much as though a man who was the victim of the worst diseases should contrive not to submit to the doctor’s penalty for his bodily transgressions and take the prescribed treatment, from a childish fear of cautery or incision, as being so painful.

greek_head: ἀλγεινόν ἢ οὐ δοκεῖ καὶ σοὶ οὕτω Ἔμοιγε
greek_tokens: algeinos h ou dokew kai sos outws egw agnoew ge ws eoika oios eimi o ugieia kai areth swma kinduneuw gar ek o nun egw omologew toioutos tis poiew kai e o dikh feugw eimi pwlos o algeinos autos kaqoraw pros de o wfelimos tuflos exw kai agnoew osos aqlios eimi mh ugihs swma mh ugihs yuxw sunoikew alla saqros kai adikos
english_head: Or do you not agree to this view of it? I do. Since he was ignorant, it would seem, of the virtue of bodily health and fitness. For it is very probable, from what we have just agreed, that something like this is done also by those who evade their due penalty, Polus; they perceive its painfulness, but are blind to its benefits, and are unaware how much more wretched than lack of health in the body it is to dwell with a soul that is not healthy, but corrupt, unjust, and unholy;

greek_head: καὶ ἀνοσίῳ ὅθεν καὶ πᾶν ποιοῦσιν ὥστε δίκην μὴ διδόναι μηδ' ἀπαλλάττεσθαι τοῦ μεγίστου κακοῦ καὶ χρήματα παρασκευαζόμενοι
greek_tokens: kai anosios oqen kai pas poiew wste dikh mh didwmi mhde apallassw o megas kakos kai xrhma paraskeuazw kai filos kai opws an eimi ws piqanos legw ei de egw alhqhs omologew eimi pwlos ara aisqanomai o sumbainw ek o logos h boulomai sullogizomai autos ei sos ge dokew ara oun sumbainw megas kakos o adikia kai o
english_head: and hence it is that they do all they can to avoid paying the penalty and being relieved of the greatest of evils, by providing themselves with money and friends and the ability to excel in persuasive speech. But if what we have agreed is true, Polus, do you observe the consequences of our argument? Or, if you like, shall we reckon them up together? Yes, if you do not mind. Then does it result that injustice and wrongdoing is the greatest evil? Yes, apparently.

greek_head: ἀδικεῖν Φαίνεταί γε Καὶ μὴν ἀπαλλαγή γε ἐφάνη τούτου τοῦ κακοῦ τὸ δίκην διδόναι Κινδυνεύει
greek_tokens: adikew fainw ge kai meis apallagh ge fainw outos o kakos o dikh didwmi kinduneuw o de ge mh didwmi emmonh o kakos nai deuteros ara eimi o kakh megeqos o adikew o de adikew mh didwmi dikh pas megas te kai proteros kakh fuw eoika ara oun ou peri outos eimi filos amfiamfisbhtew su men o arxelaos eudaimonizw o o megas adikew
english_head: And further, it appeared that paying the penalty is a relief from this evil? It looks like it. Whereas not paying it is a retention of the evil in us? Yes. Thus wrongdoing is second of evils in greatness; but to do wrong and not pay the penalty is the greatest and takes the first place among all evils. It seems so. Well now, my friend, was this the point at issue between us, that you counted Archelaus,

greek_head: δίκην οὐδεμίαν διδόντα ἐγὼ δὲ τοὐναντίον οἰόμενος εἴτε Ἀρχέλαος εἴτ' ἄλλος ἀνθρώπων ὁστισοῦν μὴ δίδωσι
greek_tokens: dikh oudeis didwmi egw de enantion oiomai eite arxelaos eite allos anqrwpos ostis mh didwmi dikh adikew outos proshkw aqlios eimi diaferontws o allos anqrwpos kai aei o adikew o adikew aqlios eimi kai o mh didwmi dikh o didwmi ou outos eimi o upo egw legw nai oukoun apodeiknumi ostis alhqhs legw fainw
english_head: who did the greatest wrong, happy because he paid no penalty, whilst I on the contrary thought that anyone—whether Archelaus or any other person you please—who pays no penalty for the wrong he has done, is peculiarly and pre-eminently wretched among men, and that it is always the wrongdoer who is more wretched than the wronged, and the unpunished than the punished? Is not this what I stated? Yes. Then has it not been proved that this was a true statement? Apparently.

greek_head: Εἶεν εἰ οὖν δὴ ταῦτα ἀληθῆ ὦ Πῶλε τίς ἡ μεγάλη χρεία ἐστὶν τῆς ῥητορικῆς δεῖ μὲν γὰρ δὴ ἐκ τῶν
greek_tokens: eien ei oun dh outos alhqhs eimi pwlos tis o megas xreos eimi o rhtorikos deomai men gar dh ek o nun omologew autos eautou malista fulassw opws mh adikew ws ikanos kakos exw ou gar panu ge ean de ge adikew h autos h allos tis os an khdw autos ekwn eimi ekeise opou ws taxistos didwmi dikh para o dikasths wsper para o iatros
english_head: Very well: so if this is true, Polus, what is the great use of rhetoric? For you see by what we have just agreed that a man must keep a close watch over himself so as to avoid wrongdoing, since it would bring a great deal of evil upon him; must he not? Certainly. But if he is guilty of wrongdoing, either himself or anyone else he may care for, he must go of his own freewill where he may soonest pay the penalty, to the judge

greek_head: σπεύδοντα ὅπως μὴ ἐγχρονισθὲν τὸ νόσημα τῆς ἀδικίας ὕπουλον τὴν ψυχὴν ποιήσει καὶ ἀνίατον ἢ πῶς λέγομεν
greek_tokens: speudw opws mh egxronizw o noshma o adikia upoulos o yuxh poihsis kai aniatos h pws legw eimi pwlos eiper o proteros menos egw omologhma ou anagkh outos ekeinos outws men sumfwnew allos de mh tis gar dh fhmi eimi swkraths epi men ara o apologeomai uper o adikia o eautou h gonaw h etairos h pais h patris adikew ou xrhsimos oudeis o rhtorikos egw eimi pwlos ei mh ei tis
english_head: as if to his doctor, with the earnest intent that the disease of his injustice shall not become chronic and cause a deep incurable ulcer in his soul. Or what are we to say, Polus, if our former conclusions stand? Must not our later ones accord with them in this way, and in this only? Yes, what else, indeed, are we to say, Socrates? Then for pleading in defence of injustice, whether it is oneself or one’s parents or friends or children or country that has done the wrong, rhetoric is of no use to

greek_head: ὑπολάβοι ἐπὶ τοὐναντίον κατηγορεῖν δεῖν μάλιστα μὲν ἑαυτοῦ ἔπειτα δὲ καὶ τῶν οἰκείων καὶ τῶν ἄλλων ὃς ἂν

english_head: he must submit to the rod; if fetters, to their grip; if a fine, to its payment; if banishment, to be banished; or if death, to die; himself to be the first accuser either of himself or of his relations, and to employ his rhetoric for the purpose of so exposing their iniquities that they may be relieved of that greatest evil, injustice. english_head: Such are the purposes, as it seems to me, Polus, for which rhetoric is useful, since to him who has no intention of doing wrong it is, I consider, of no great use, if indeed there is any use in it at all; for in our previous argument it was nowhere to be found. Tell me, Chaerephon, is Socrates in earnest over this, or only joking? To my thinking, Callicles, prodigiously in earnest: still, there is nothing like asking him.

english_head: Upon my word, just what I want to do. Tell me, Socrates, are we to take you as serious just now, or joking? For if you are serious and what you say is really true, must not the life of us human beings have been turned upside down, and must we not be doing quite the opposite, it seems, of what we ought to do? Callicles, if men had not certain feelings, each common to one sort of people,

And this, look you, is the clever trick you have devised for our undoing in your discussions: when a man states anything according to convention you slip according to nature into your questions; and again, if he means nature, you imply convention. In the present case, for instance, of doing and suffering wrong, when Polus was speaking of what is conventionally fouler, you followed it up in the sense of what is naturally so.
